data.title: British American Tobacco Outshines Philip Morris As A Dividend Buy
data.description:
BTI vs. PM stock: Previous thesis and new developments

I last wrote on British American Tobacco (NYSE:BTI) in April and Philip Morris International (NYSE:PM) in March. My last BTI article was titled "British American Tobacco Needs Time To Consolidate (Technical Analysis)," and the PM article was titled "Philip Morris: Wall Street Buy Rating Vs. Insider Sales." Both articles were oriented toward the near term. I rated BTI as a hold based on the technical trading patterns at that time, and PM as a sell based on the latest disclosure of insider transactions.

Since then, a few new developments have evolved, both in terms of their own business fundamentals and also in terms of macroscopic geopolitics. Catalysts in the first bucket included the business updates outlined in their earnings reports and investor presentations released after those articles. As for catalysts in the second bucket, the tariff drama that has started on April 2 is the most important one. At this point, I don’t believe there's sufficient information to make any intelligent projections of the outcome of trade negotiations, let alone the potential impact of the outcome on either BTI or PM.

The above uncertainties motivated me to look past the near-term issues and examine both stocks from a long-term perspective. In particular, this article will examine both stocks via the window of their latest dividend declarations due to a few considerations. First, dividends are an important factor for many investors’ interests in BTI and PM to start with. Second, given the long track record of dividend at both companies, I consider the payout as a more accurate metric for their true economic earnings. And finally, perhaps most importantly, past experiences have taught me that the more uncertain the time, the more I should focus on the variables that have the least ambiguity. Dividends are certainly one of the few metrics that involve no degree of subjective interpretation and/or ambiguity.

In the rest of this article, I will assess BTI and PM’s latest fundamentals based on their dividends. The assessment will involve a few perspectives that are not part of the routine check of many dividend investors (such as share repurchases and/or margins) as you can see from my following scorecard. After a comprehensive comparison, my verdict is that BTI offers a much more attractive return/risk ratio than PM, leading me to maintain my sell rating on PM while adjusting my rating on BTI to buy.

1. BTI vs. PM stock: Dividend record

The subsequent assessment will follow an approach that I’ve used in the past to screen dividend growth stocks. The approach is a collection of criteria gathered from my reading of Peter Lynch, which have been detailed in an earlier article. To keep this article within a reasonable length, I will minimize the background of the approach and only quotes what’s absolutely needed as we go.

The approach starts, of course, with establishing a track record as payouts. Lynch’s rule of thumb is a track record of stable payouts for ~20 years or more, which shows the business’ staying power through different economic cycles. Both BTI and PM stocks satisfy these requirements, in my view. Although two notes are worth pointing out. First, Philip Morris only became an independent publicly held company when it was spun off from the parent company, Altria Group, in 2008. Thus, many websites (like the screenshot provided below) only quote its dividend historical since then. Second, BTI’s dividends are declared in British pounds, but the growth reported by many websites is computed in US dollars. Thus, the exchange rates fluctuations could distort its growth rates (and also years of consecutive growth).

With the above, I consider both companies satisfy the first requirement and assign 1 point to each for this round.

2. BTI vs. PEF stock: Valuation and PEGY ratios

As for valuation, the simple P/E ratio can provide misleading information, or at least partial information for dividend stocks for the insights Lynch provided below:

PEG refers to the price-to-earnings growth ratio. He also introduced a modified version of the PEG ratio for dividends stocks. The modified ratio is called the PEGY ratio and is computed as the P/E ratio divided by the sum of the earnings growth rate and dividend yield. The concept is simple yet effective. If the dividends are high enough, investors should not expect (and do not need either) a high growth rate anymore. Just like the PEG ratio, Lynch favors PEGY ratios below 1x.

As such, the table below summarizes my calculation of the PEGY ratios for BTI and PM. These results were based on a few inputs. First, for growth rates, I use BTI and PM’s dividend growth rate in the past three years, which are 2.08% for BTI and 2.62% for PM to approximate the growth rate of their owners’ earnings. For their FWD dividend yield, I calculated it based on their latest payout declarations at the price of this writing, which are $1.35 per share for PM and $0.7491 per share for BTI.

Based on these inputs, PM’s PEGY ratio is 4.24, far above the 1x ideal pick. In contrast, BTI’s PEGY ratio is only 1.12x, quite close to the ideal level. As such, BTI is the undisputed leader on this front.

3. BTI vs. PM: Dividend safety

Dividend safety needs little emphasis for all dividend investors. As seen from the table shown in the forgoing section, PM’s current dividend payout ratio is around 79.4%, which is quite consistent with its historical average of around 80% (which again echoes with my earlier comment about the importance of dividends for PM investors). As for BTI, my estimate for its 2025 payout ratio is about 64.4%, which is also consistent with its historical average level of around 63%. This estimate is based on an FWD payout of $0.7491 per BTI share as just mentioned and an FWD EPS projection of $4.65 per share.

Given that BTI’s payout ratio is noticeably lower than PM both in terms of historical averages and also current levels, I assign another point to BTI in this round.

4. BTI vs. PM stock: Share repurchases

Many (or most) dividend investors invest in dividend stocks for the cash distribution, and share buybacks often do not get their attention. However, this is an equally important aspect because:

Share buybacks are just as valuable for total returns as cash dividends (even more so if you consider tax implications). Yet, it is overlooked by many dividend investors. Plus, share buybacks are a strong indicator of financial strength (that a company has excess cash flow beyond capital expenditures and dividend obligations) and also management's confidence in their own business.

In the case of BTI and PM, BTI is just a much more consistent and larger buyer of its own shares than PM, as you can tell from the next two figures. To wit, BTI has been consistently buying back its shares in recent years. According to its latest cash flow statement (see the second figure below), it spent more than $208 million toward shares repurchases in the last quarter of 2024 alone. In contrast, PM had stopped its repurchasing activities in recent years.

As such, I believe BTI is also the winner on this front.

5. BTI vs. PM stock: Profit margins

Profitability metrics are not part of the analysis for many dividend investors, as they are not directly related to dividend payouts. However, better profit margins offer a thicker buffer against uncertainties. I think profitability metrics have heightened relevance now given the trade conflicts we are experiencing. No matter how these uncertainties play out, the stock with the thicker margin tends to weather better, which in turn translates into an better dividend safety and dividend growth potential.

In the case of BTI and PM, the good news is that both feature superb profitability, as you can see from the following statistics. Both of them boast very high gross profit margin (GPM). PM has a GPM of 65.68%, and BTI's GPM is even higher at 82.33%. As we move downstream in their income statement, BTI and PM show a very similar EBIT Margin of around 37%. Finally, in terms of net margin, PM’s reading of 19.8% is better than BTI’s 11.8%.

All told, I call this around a tie and assign one point to each as I consider that A) both their margins are terrific under a broader scheme of things, and B) their margins are also quite comparable to each other.

Other risks and final thoughts

To conclude, the goal of this reexamination on BTI and PM are motivated by their latest financial reports in their earnings reports and also the developments of the trade uncertainties. As mentioned, I consider the impacts of the trade uncertainties unknowable at this point, and thus urge investors to focus on the return vectors with the least amount of ambiguity, such as dividends. After a holistic comparison, my final scorecard (pasted again below) points to BTI as a better-rounded package featuring high yield, better valuation, lower payout, and strong profitability metrics.

In terms of downside risks, both PM and BTI face largely the same set of generic risks, such as the secular demand decline for their smokable products, regulatory risks, etc. Risks particular to BTI include the uncertain path ahead of its New Categories division. On the positive side, the demand for its modern oral products like Velo has been quite robust lately. However, on the negative side, the profits from these new products are still a relatively minor profit stream for the company overall. The recent rise of illicit single-use vapor products is another risk worth monitoring and will probably continue to hurt BIT until more effective enforcement can tackle illicit products.

Overall, the consumer staple sector (where both PM and BTI belong) is neutrally valued in our model. To wit, the screenshot below is taken from our market dashboard, a tool our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) developed to obtain a macroscopic view of the market (it’s downloadable as a Google sheet). As seen, the staple sector, approximated by XLP, current sits squarely in the mid of various market sectors when ranked by the dividend yield Z-score. We hope this overview better contextualizes this article. When a whole sector is attractively valued, it is often better – and simpler – to just buy the sector in our experience. But in this case, it's not and thus investors do have to examine the individual stocks more closely and our examination reveals BTI as a strong dividend stock than PM.

data.title: Oshkosh Corporation: Balancing Near Term Headwind With Long-Term Potential
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Company Overview

Oshkosh Corporation (NYSE:OSK), founded in 1913 with headquarters in Wisconsin, is a global industrial company specializing in heavy machine and equipment manufacturing and development that is customized for specific industrial usage, such as postal, firefighting, refuse & recycling collection, construction, aviation, and defense.

Analysis

Oshkosh is caught between a strong line-up of innovative product development and slower sales in the US due to the current economic environment. In Q1 this year, the company experienced a sales decline in two of its three reportable segments, Access, Vocational, and Defense. Its largest segment, Access, which provides access and material handling equipment for use in equipment rental companies, construction and manufacturing industries, and home improvement shops, accounted for 48% of its full-year sales in 2024. Its smallest segment, the Defense segment, which sustains specialty vehicles and mobility systems for the DoD (US Department of Defense), which provides US Postal Service vehicles and other Heavy to Medium Tactical and Caliber Weapons systems, accounted for 20% of total full-year sales in 2024. Together, these two segments had a decline in sales of up to 13% in Q1 2025. The decline was partially offset by a 12.2% growth in the Vocational segment, which accounted for 31% of its full-year sales in 2024. The Q1 net sales in the end were a 9.1% decline. The vocational segment focuses on providing airports with firefighting vehicles, airport ground support, gate equipment, and baggage/facility services, as well as other commercial or municipal customers with truck-mounted cranes, refuse and recycling collection vehicles, and concrete mixers.

Management noted that the negative impact particularly came from "a softer market condition for Access equipment in North America" combined with some "catch-up adjustment" in the Defense segment. Its operating income was brought down by 30.3% YoY, and EPS was lowered by 33.6%.

But to put it in perspective, Oshkosh is still at one of its strongest revenue generations in the past 15 years, and this level has only been briefly reached post-2008 financial crisis, especially after the sluggish sales from 2012 to 2020. Similarly, its EBITDA and net income are still hovering at a high level compared to where they were just two years ago.

There are reasons to believe this level of topline can be sustained or exceeded because what's driving this patch of growth is its innovation. It has three major product developments: electric heavy machines, autonomy, and embedded intelligence. The company has been in the research and development of EV for about 25 years, according to its website. It is currently offering McNeilus Volterra ZSL electric refuse collection vehicle, U.S. Postal Service Next Generation Delivery Vehicle (NGDV), Pierce Volterra Pumper (an electric fire truck), Striker Volterra Electric Vehicle (for airport operation), Electric Front Discharge Concrete Placement Vehicle, etc. The USPS NGDV rollout is set to happen over the next ten years with a delivery order of 50k to 160k vehicles. Some municipalities have already adopted the electric fire truck, such as the City of Madison in Wisconsin. These vehicles are all highly customized, and most of them are the first of their kind. The potential demand is enormous, and the adoption of the municipalities and heavy equipment rental companies in the US is only at the beginning.

Oshkosh's autonomy & active safety development is in combination of its proprietary intelligent products. Its advanced telematics system serves as the sensors for the road/working condition, and augmented reality helps the operator to visualize the environment. These lay out the foundation for its robotic perception technologies, such as LIDAR, RADAR, cameras, and GPS, to incorporate AI into its prototype products. The image below showcases how its Lidar technology is used to detect and classify objects in order for the vehicle to operate "smartly" with autonomy. Embedding intelligent, autonomous systems in its EVs would increase its product competitiveness to draw more demand. The Collision Avoidance Mitigation System (CAMS) showcased at the FDIC show in Q1 is classified under the Vocational segment.

Oshkosh experienced a slowdown in its JLG sales in 2024, which are mostly made up of lifts and access equipment (see the image below). The EV rollout also includes lifts as well as garbage trucks, fire trucks, airport ground transport vehicles, and delivery trucks, along with the new USPS mail truck.

The future trend is to adopt autonomous equipment run on sustainable fuel with an intelligent system to reduce energy footprint and increase efficiency. Take autonomous construction equipment alone as an example; this area alone could experience 10.1% CAGR in the next four years. Oshkosh's product lineup is laying the ground ahead for a future takeoff.

As its management pointed out, Oshkosh has a globally diverse supply chain, and is actively deploying mitigation measures that could bring the negative $1.00 blow due to tariffs to its adj. EPS down to $0.5. In answering related questions, its CFO Matt Field said,

Most of the cost elements would be Access in terms of how the costs flow through on tariffs. The cost offsets those will be broad-based across the company.

So far, these cost-saving measures have been helping to maintain its gross, operating, and EBITDA margins, which are still at the upper range of historic levels.

Investors interested in Oshkosh should have a short-term view of its immediate headwinds as well as a long-term vision of its potential.

Financial Overview & Valuation

Oshkosh received several downgrades in April after reporting its earnings, and within a month, it has risen by 30% and reached 4 out of 6 of these targets. Note that none of the brokers' ratings is meant for a one-month investment horizon. It goes to show that sometimes the Street can be too reactionary to short-term changes.

The pessimism from the Street post a discount to Oshkosh's valuation. Oshkosh has the lowest P/E at 19x compared to Federal Signal (FSS), Allison Transmission (ALSN), Atmus Filtration Technologies (ATMU) and Terex Corporation (TEX). The decline of P/E ratio is due to the price stagnation in comparison to its EBITDA growth, which went from $390 million in 2022 to now $1.1 billion. And its P/E ratio was almost 40-50x in 2022.

Similarly, its topline growth was heavily discounted as well. It also has one of the lowest Price/Sales ratios, while its revenue has increased by almost 30% within the past three years.

By price/operating income ratio, it is only 7x, while all its peers are in the teens. The dismissive and pessimism priced in this stock has positioned it to have a better risk/reward profile in relative terms. Given the medium-term growth potential brought on by its innovation, the short-term headwind actually produced a better entry point for investors. We think the stock is undervalued and is a buy at the current price.

The risk to our thesis is the trade war will drag on beyond 2025 and Oshkosh will continue having to resort to cost-cutting measures that might impact its long-term growth potential. But given some of its long-term contracts, such as with USPS, will come into place in 2026, and the low leverage ratio the company has, we think it can mitigate the downside risks and strive to reach its long-term potential.

Conclusion

We analyzed Oshkosh's short-term headwinds and its long-term potential, and factored them into its valuation. The stock looks undervalued given that its growth extended from the past few years is still intact. Although the short-term momentum pointed to the downside, we think the company is resilient enough to weather the rough patch. Interested investors can scoop up some shares at the current prices.

data.title: Salesforce's Q1 Shocker: I See No Dead Money Here
data.description:
Introduction

I first covered Salesforce, Inc. (NYSE:CRM) stock in August 2024 with a "Buy" rating, arguing that despite some clear challenges that I saw at the time, the stock was poised for nice medium-term performance. It was the case for the next few months after that call, but eventually CRM lost its superior momentum, and it's currently trading flat since that very first "Buy" rating of mine.

My most recent article came out in February 2025, where I reiterated my bullish stance and argued that CRM should have beaten the then-upcoming Q4 FY2025 consensus expectations, and it did beat on the bottom line, but missed on the top line. Since then, CRM stock has lost ~20% of its nominal value.

As I'm writing these lines, CRM is falling again, following its just-released Q1 FY2026 earnings. Let's dive in to get a better understanding of what's going on with Salesforce's business internally, and also answer the question of whether it's worth buying the dip now?

Reviewing Salesforce's Q1 FY2025 Numbers

On the surface, we see that Salesforce's Q1 FY2026 earnings beat the consensus: The sales came in at $9.83 billion (+7.62% YoY), beating by less than 1%, and EPS came in at $2.58 (+5.73% YoY, a positive surprise of ~1.31%), which was one of the lowest beats of the past few years, according to Seeking Alpha premium data.

CRM saw a "very strong growth in our small and medium market business", where both segments achieved "strong double-digit new bookings growth", according to the management's commentary during the earnings call, and that speaks of better business diversification in the future period (at least in my understanding). I also liked their margins (non-GAAP EBIT margin was ~32.3% for the quarter), and cash flow generation (the CFO amount was a healthy $6.5 billion), while regarding CRPO ("Current Performance Obligations") went up by 13% YoY and reached $29.6 billion.

As Goldman Sachs analysts noted in their post-earnings review (proprietary source, May 2025), that dynamic in CRPO showed "the most net new dollars added YoY since Q1 FY2023", so I believe that's why CRM's management decided to raise its revenue guidance by ~$400 million at the high end, now targeting $41.3 billion.

We have $60.9 billion in RPO, up 13% year-over-year, and we had exceptional performance with CRPO growing year-over-year in constant currency. And we're raising our fiscal year '26 guidance by $400 million to $41.3 billion at the high end of the range.

Source: Seeking Alpha's Earnings Call Insights

What's important in understanding CRM's prospects: Their Agentforce managed to secure >8,000 deals in just 2 fiscal quarters since launch, with 4,000 of those being paid customers (according to the management), and it has already surpassed $100 million in Annual Order Value (aka AOV), which is faster than any product in Salesforce's history. The combined ARR for CRM's Data Cloud and Agentforce has already exceeded $1 billion, growing more than 120% on a YoY basis, and I believe it's going to accelerate further as both products haven't had enough time yet to get any meaningful synergetic benefits.

And now turning to perhaps the most important announcement - Salesforce's acquisition of Informatica for $8 billion. The target company is focusing on data management and ETL processing, which are crucial parts of any data analysis process in general. The CFO added more color by saying that CRM "expects to achieve accretion on non-GAAP operating margin, non-GAAP EPS and free cash flow by year 2 post close", so it's assumingly by early fiscal year 2027. While investors wait, CRM's management noted that they target for 34% non-GAAP EBIT margin in FY2026, so the M&A-related costs shouldn't eat away at their core business.

Unlike most tech firms in other niches, Salesforce keeps hiring: They want to hire another 1,000-2,000 salespeople, "aiming for a 22% overall distribution capacity growth by year-end", according to the CRO's commentary on the call, so I think this indirectly speaks of the overall demand strength as this projection co-exists with the EBIT target of 34% for FY2026.

Although on one of the charts above, we saw that 5 out of 6 Wall Street analysts covering the stock decided to lower their estimates for fiscal Q2 FY2026, a more granular view reveals that not everything is so bad as the MoM trend is actually positive for CRM's EPS:

Probably the market was spooked by the new M&A deal as it might interrupt their generous shareholder return practice. For context, in Q1, Salesforce returned >$3 billion through share repurchases and dividends, bringing the total cash returned since the program's inception to >$24 billion. So that $8 billion might delay the continuation of such rewards in the future. Also, their Q2 CRPO growth guidance of 10% nominal (or 9% YoY in constant currency) might have also tempered some initial excitement (that's my guess based on Goldman Sachs' commentary).

Anyway, I recall that earlier this year, I called Agentforce's early promise and the massive potential of the AI agents market, and this time, CRM proved that my assumptions were not unfounded. I think that the rapid scaling of Agentforce and Data Cloud, with now the strategic addition of Informatica, should bring in stable growth for the next few years (at least), and CRM will likely further beat the market's EPS consensus numbers by 1-3% on a quarterly basis.

CRM Stock Valuation Update

CRM is up by >33% over the past 5 years, which isn't a lot for a tech stock, considering the Invesco QQQ Trust's (QQQ) total return of 133.4% for the same period of time. Anyway, we can correlate CRM's underperformance with a bunch of factors, with the main one being the firm's growth deceleration during the post-COVID period. As the stock went up by 33%, its P/E multiple (on a TTM basis) managed to fall by over 46%, now trading at almost 41x. What's interesting here: On a FWD basis, CRM is priced almost twice as cheaper, at ~23x:

The market's consensus, which CRM keeps beating (even though at a declining magnitude), says that the firm's EPS will be growing at about 14% annually for the next 4 years (up to FY2030), so we can't say that CRM will lack business expansion going forward. Considering their consistent buybacks, which I believe will be there even if they acquire new targets, theoretically speaking, the stock should be at least retaining its valuation premium. If so, we can assume that by FY2027, CRM's P/E should be close to 25x, and it's not that optimistic.

So, at 25x earnings by FY2027 and applying a slight premium on top of the current consensus estimate (of just 2%), I get a 12-18 months price target of $322.6/share, which is 24% above the current stock price.

The Bottom Line

Again, as I noted in my previous article, I may be wrong on multiple fronts regarding CRM's prospects. For example, if their current growth in Agentforce doesn't hold, they'll lose an important lever for supporting elevated growth rates. The initial success in the Agentforce business may be short-lived, and the synergetic effect with Informatica may not come at all, so I should warn investors to keep that in mind and avoid boosting their expectations of the potential positive impact just yet.

Despite that, I generally liked CRM's Q1 numbers. Yes, you won't see 30-40% growth in top line at 50-70% operating margins here, but their "AI Agentforce story" seems to progress well, and it looks promising so far. The undervaluation of 24% that I arrived at today may not seem sufficient for some growth investors, but CRM is more for GARP investors, I believe, and the actual undervaluation might turn out to be higher if the current trends continue to evolve throughout calendar 2025-2026.

I decided to keep my "Buy" in place for now.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Nvidia Stock's Post-Earnings Bump Might Not Last
data.description:
Market-leading chipmaker Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) walked up to its first quarter (Q1) earnings for its Fiscal Year (FY) 2026 on the 28th of May with a warning. This was that its total revenues would be taking a hit after the U.S. administration continued with a largely bipartisan effort enacted over the course of the past five years to restrict sales of advanced technology to China by including its "China-specific" H20 chips with immediate effect.

In the fortnight leading up to the Q1 2026 earnings release, the company's stock essentially held steady around the $124 level attained on the 14th of May.

Traded volumes shrunk back from the highs seen in early April after President Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs were announced and followed by tightening controls on technology sales to China. Given the rise seen, traded volumes could be considered cautiously bullish.

By reporting revenue of $44.1 billion for Q1 2026 versus Bloomberg-compiled analyst estimates of $43.3 billion, and delivering adjusted earnings per share (EPS) of $0.96 (excluding the "charge" from the loss of H20 chip sales to China) versus an estimate of $0.93, the company is adjudged by analysts as having passed muster.

However, the bottom line is definitely taking a hit in trends.

Trend Drilldown

Note: As of the time of writing, the company hasn't furnished the more detailed 10-K statement submitted to the SEC. Thus, the trend doesn't have some of the details presented in the previous article about Nvidia's Q4 2025 earnings.

As per the company, a little over 10% of its revenue in Q1 2026 – $4.6 billion – was from H20 sales to China before the export restrictions kicked in and caused a nearly equivalent charge of $4.5 billion.

Nonetheless, early revenue trends seem encouraging – at least at first blush.

If trends were to continue, the company would have realized a 36% growth in revenue in FY 2026, which is a third of what was achieved in the previous FY. Meanwhile, the cost of revenue is already trending to be 112% higher for FY 2026, which is somewhat consistent with what was witnessed in the previous FY. CFO Colette Kress' commentary states that this is due to higher compensation expenses (which would likely be reflected in the Stock-Based Compensation figures that are currently not available) as well development costs for new products (which would have been reflected in R&D costs, also not currently available).

Net Income per share is currently trending to close FY 2026 with a paltry 4% growth, which is a mighty fall from the triple-digit percentage growth seen over the past two FYs.

The company's "Compute and Network" segment continues to grow on the back of data center demand.

Graphics cards – once its mainstay that endeared the company's products among gamers all over the world – remains on a decline. Also, the "OEM and Other" revenue category – which encompasses robotics, embedded systems, and AI-powered products for industrial automation – registered a 12% quarter-on-quarter decrease. If this trend continues, the company is essentially set to be mostly (if not nearly wholly) dependent on data center demand for its cash flows.

The language of the earnings call heavily emphasized its new achievements on data centers: the company's Blackwell platform was reported to have set records in AI-driven inference results by delivering up to 30 times higher throughput and Blackwell cloud instances are now reported as being available on Amazon's (AMZN) AWS, Alphabet's (GOOG) Google Cloud, Microsoft's (MSFT) Azure, and Oracle's (ORCL) Cloud Infrastructure. Also announced were a host of initiatives to build AI factories in Saudi Arabia and infrastructure clusters in Abu Dhabi, plans to work on an AI factory supercomputer in collaboration with Foxconn (OTCPK:FXCOF) and the Government of Taiwan, as well as a research center in Japan.

In Conclusion

The company estimates that Q2 2026 revenue will effectively run on par with Q1's at $45 billion. Considering the fact that this excludes sales to China, this represents a net 10% increase in global demand, ex-China. In terms of top-line trends, this is a net positive, despite not being quite as dramatic as those witnessed in prior quarters. Gross margins, on the other hand, shot up 10% to 71% after the loss of China business and raw material obligations piled up. As the company recognizes a further $8 billion in Q2 over the loss of business, this means that the 71% margin will continue to weigh down earnings.

CEO Jensen Huang stated that the $50 billion China market is effectively closed to the U.S. industry as a whole and that the company is currently exploring limited ways to compete in it. However, given the highly focused nature of export restrictions, Nvidia likely won't be able to compete in it for a while.

A lot of the valuation premium that Nvidia's stock exhibited in price ratio terms over its peers in the semiconductor industry came from its massive top- and bottom-line growth as it made inroads in data centers all around the world (including China). With bottom-line trends likely affected by new product rollouts, inventory reordering and relatively stable demand from the rest of the world, a correction in the valuation premium to the tune of 10-15% is quite likely over the course of Q2 earnings.

Regardless of CEO Huang's statements, the company isn't massively affected, at least in the present by the loss of the China market. However, the forward growth outlook in the ticker is a different matter: as growth softens, the sky-high valuation will be tested and perhaps found wanting. There will likely be tactical trading opportunities galore on both the upside as well as the downside for the next three months (if not further). Growth-oriented investors, on the other hand, might have to make do with overall single-digit percentage bottom-line growth for the duration of FY 2026.

data.title: Trulieve: Well-Positioned In A Struggling Industry
data.description:
Trulieve (OTCQX:TCNNF) is one of many cannabis companies, one that may have some opportunity as the industry is challenged by excessive debt coming due. With a healthier balance sheet, Trulieve could move to capture market share, depending on how these debt restructurings occur, while also reducing its own interest expense. Because it remains priced low even for very modest growth, I believe the stock makes for a reasonable Buy.

November Stock Dive

One of the first things to consider is the major dive that TCNNF's price took last fall.

The stock has lost more than half its value over the past year. The sharpest part of the drop occurred early in November. What led to this? The primary cause was the results of the 2024 election. The presidential race wasn't the key; it was Florida. The state had a referendum that would have upgraded cannabis's legal use from medical to recreational, but this was rejected by the voters.

While Trulieve operates in eight states, the table above shows that their business is primarily concentrated in the state of Florida. As the federal ban severely limits cannabis companies in developing their interstate supply chains, most of their Floridian assets are only useful for the production and sale of medical cannabis in that state. Had the referendum passed, the earning potential of these assets would have increased significantly, and the market appears to have been pricing in a win that did not occur.

The stock price was near $12 prior to the election and now sits close to $4, so some might wonder if the lower price is fair for the current state and undervalued for the upside.

Financial State

The first matter with any specific cannabis stock is if it can survive the debt crisis that plagues most of the industry.

Above, I've indicated the key parts of Trulieve's latest balance sheet. It indicates a liquid position of about $328M. Many of the key current liabilities are well below that. Their long-term debt in their two items total about $476M.

Looking at the constituent maturities, all of the private placement notes mature in October of next year. The current cash is roughly sufficient here. The remaining notes have staggered maturities no earlier than November 2027.

Q1's cash flow statement also show it generating about $30M in cash, supporting its already very liquid position.

The annual data show us that Trulieve was able to clear $100M in free cash flow for 2024 and 2023. If this continues, the current cash balance and cash flow should be sufficient for their own debt hurdle, which isn't something a lot of cannabis companies can say right now.

Future Outlook

Will that occur, however? I think that it will, and it largely owes to its concentration in Florida, a medical-only market, which doesn't reflect the impacts of the adult-use market very much. Jesse Redmond of Leef Brands (OTCQB:LEEEF) shared some insights on Seeking Alpha's The Cannabis Investing Podcast remarked in their episode last April:

But what we're having at '25 is Florida didn't happen, and how disappointing was that? Pennsylvania didn't happen yet. I still think that does over a reasonable time frame, but no guarantee that happens in '25. In Minnesota, it looks like that's getting kicked to the spring of '26. So what we're left with are the 24 adult new states that we have, and we're just seeing those margins generally compress.

Adult-use is a different market than medical, subject more to discretionary consumer habits. Rising interest rates slowed the economy and, with it, consumer spending, which affects retailers' ability to price effectively. By comparison, Trulieve's concentration in medical Florida has allowed it to maintain healthy gross margins of about 60% from not over-extending. While the ballot defeat snuffed out a lot of their upside, they still were not indebted more than the current business could afford to repay. They weren't betting on a strong consumer that turned out to be weak.

If Trulieve can strike the right balance between retiring and refinancing debt, they have room to capture share in non-Floridian markets, potentially scooping up assets from distressed operators at better prices. As this will depend heavily on the details of how these operators restructure their debts with lenders, it's an opportunity for Trulieve, albeit shrouded in uncertainty.

It's also worth noting that retiring the debt would boost earnings in and of itself. Interest expense fell from $81.6M in 2023 to $62.2M in 2024. There's plenty of room to get that down and leave more for shareholders over time.

Let's consider what kind of value one can get with modest assumptions, however, through a Discounted Cash Flow Model:

  • Baseline FCF: $100M
  • CAGR over next decade: 5%
  • Terminal multiple: 10

Once again, FCF has been over $100M the last two years, so I believe it can continue to deliver that after building itself up over the last few years and still mainly depending on a medical market. The CAGR and terminal multiple simply assume that modest growth can occur as long as they don't make the same mistakes that other cannabis operators did and move on reasonable opportunities.

Priced for a 10% discount rate (typical return of a market index), this suggests that TCNNF is undervalued by about 45%, as the current price is $4.05 on an intrinsic value of $7.37.

The primary risks include continued uncertainty about the federal level. The Trump Administration remains ambiguous on the matter of rescheduling, and the removal of the 280E tax burden may not come for some time. While Florida seems to have established that it's okay with medical cannabis but not adult-use, there are risks to being concentrated in this market, and they don't have to pertain to state-level cannabis regulation. Weakness in the Floridian economy or powerful hurricanes that could damage Trulieve's dispensaries and facilities are worth considering as well.

Conclusion

Overall, Trulieve appears to have sufficient liquidity and cash flow to handle its own debt. Free cash flow can grow with opportunistic market share capture and deleveraging. It doesn't require them to bear too much risk, and they are in a position to be picky if they want. Because the valuation remains low, I think the risks of cannabis and their geographic concentration are worth it, and that is why I rate TCNNF a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Zeta Global: Finding Opportunity In Risk
data.description:
Zeta Global Holdings Corp. (NYSE:ZETA) is an AI-powered marketing technology company, enabling customer acquisition and engagement for enterprises and brands, by leveraging proprietary data and predictive analytics across digital channels. The promise of an expanding adoption of its Zeta Marketing Platform (ZMP), continued integration of generative AI, and increasing demand for personalized and data-driven marketing solutions looks a good bet. More so after the sell-off from the highs of Q4 2024.

Revenue Claims and Data Privacy Issues

The sell-off from November 2024 highs was triggered by claims of false financial reporting using two-way contracts (overlapping vendors and customers) and data privacy issues (farming data illegally), among other issues. Zeta has since offered a rebuttal to those claims. Data privacy and farming are common allegations that plague Customer Data Platform (CDP) players. Data leaks, regulatory scrutiny, and compliance issues will be a permanent risk Zeta will operate under. The financial misrepresentations have been clarified as audited and complying. Overall, the response appears strong and addresses the concerns fully. However, market memory will take time to reset. Whether that presents an opportunity or investors should for legal clarity to emerge is difficult to predict. Certainly, this legal risk will weigh on my thesis, and if the other pointers find the case bullish, will at least lead me to take on smaller position sizes.

Financials and Balance Sheet

Revenue growth has been stupendous over the past few years now. Management guidance has also been positive, with guidance being upgraded to hit 23% YoY growth in top line in 2025 at the midpoint. The only concern I see is the slowing growth after a landmark 2024. That, along with macro pressures on marketing spend cuts, could pressurize top line growth for Zeta. I will keep an eye on whether this dips below the 15-20% range as a red flag for re-evaluation. Right now, I am aligned to a hold ZETA on revenue growth.

Gross margins also appear healthy. As its SaaS platform scales and efficiencies improve, there is potential for margins to expand to about 65-70% at the maximum. Higher margins like that seen in some other SaaS companies with more scalable offerings cannot be expected of Zeta with its offerings based on a mix of software, data (not so scalable costs) and customized services. So Zeta ticks the margin boxes as of now.

Zeta's operating leverage appears to be improving too. The growing Free Cash Flow points towards less cash burn as it scales. However the FCF to revenue is weak, reflecting ongoing investments in growth and infrastructure. This contrasts with more mature SaaS peers that typically generate 20–30%+ FCF margins, signaling Zeta is still in an early efficiency phase.

Business Fundamentals

Customer retention is looking decent. As mentioned in Q1 2025, Zeta has been reporting revenue retention rates of 111%-114$ between 2021 and 2024 and aims to retain similar levels going forward.

Zeta Global is continually enhancing its proprietary identity graph and AI platform to maintain a competitive edge in marketing technology. Recent initiatives include integrating LiveIntent’s identity graph to improve authenticated identity resolution, launching AI Agent Studio for sophisticated marketing automation and introducing the Zeta Media Engine powered by Snowflake for privacy-first, data-enriched programmatic marketing. I find the continuous learning bias good for long-term prospects. However, as a lot of companies do in the era of AI surge, Zeta markets its AI strategy aggressively - more than what current monetization evidences.

Customer wins are also on track. Zeta serves 44% of the Fortune 500.

Competition is a significant risk. Zeta is competing in a highly contested space where Adobe and Salesforce dominate, and newer AI-driven challengers are gaining traction, posing a real threat to market share and pricing power. So far, ZETA's growth has not been impacted, but investors will need to watch whether any competitive drag is creeping in over time. Otherwise, I am relatively bullish on digital marketing and CDP. The expanding addressable market should be able to support Zeta's expansion despite competition.

Zeta is also actively addressing share dilution going ahead. Share count dilution is expected to moderate, with forecasts of 4% to 6% in 2025 and 3% to 4% in 2026.

Valuations

Looking at the EV to Sales forward ratio, Zeta trades at a discount to peers, due to smaller scale, weaker FCF margins, and perceived governance risks, but that also implies valuation upside if execution continues. It is further interesting that currently valuations are towards the lower end of Zeta's valuation spectrum.

Investment Advice

Given Zeta’s strong growth profile, expanding platform adoption and sound fundamentals, the long-term thesis remains positive. ZETA operates in a structurally growing market (digital marketing and CDP), has carved out meaningful enterprise traction, and trades at a valuation discount relative to peers, offering upside potential if execution continues. However, legal overhang from past allegations, macro-related marketing budget pressures, and rising competitive intensity from incumbents and AI-native challengers warrant caution.

For investors with moderate risk tolerance, Zeta may qualify as a small speculative Buy, especially after the post Q4 2024 reset. Position sizing should reflect the unresolved legal risks and lower FCF efficiency. Investors already holding the stock can remain patient, while new entrants may consider layering in gradually, watching for confirmation of margin expansion, customer retention and legal resolution before materially increasing exposure.

data.title: Pure Storage: Losing Patience As Margins Wane (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
To say that the Q1 earnings season has been volatile is no understatement. Against the backdrop of very choppy stock markets and consistently shifting headlines on tariffs, investors have digested earnings reports with far more trepidation than usual.

Pure Storage (NYSE:PSTG), unfortunately, has been dealing with fundamental issues beyond just the current quarter. The company's Q1 print failed to materially convince investors that its margins are back on the right track. Pure Storage remains down more than 10% for the year, and in my view, the stock will have a difficult hill to climb to recover to former heights.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Pure Storage in March, when the stock was trading in the low $40s. At the time, Pure Storage had just crashed after its Q4 results showcased a surprising and sharp drop in its gross margin profile, owing to rising flash costs and the need to keep pricing competitive. Today, shares of Pure Storage are higher, eliminating much of the valuation safety net that I saw before, while the company faces new pressure on its margins: rising R&D costs to keep up in a competitive, yet largely commoditized industry. With this in mind, I'm dropping Pure Storage back down to a sell rating.

To me, these are the core risks that Pure Storage faces:

  • Pure Storage's margin pressure is a symptom of a highly commoditized industry. Data storage has dozens of competitors, ranging from DIY data centers to fully managed cloud solutions. Though Pure Storage tries to focus on its value-add through software, its recent declines in gross margin show an inability for the company to raise prices even in spite of rising input costs for fear of losing customers.
  • Is AI really a demand tailwind for Pure Storage? COVID proved to be a bigger bump for Pure Storage than AI, and at its peak in 2021/2022 Pure Storage was showcasing quarterly revenue growth of 40%+. One of the main inputs of AI and large language models is giant troves of data that need to be readily and quickly queried: so why isn't Pure Storage growing faster than its current low teens pace? Again, we fall back to the fact that data storage is still highly competitive, so in spite of companies' need to invest in AI and data infrastructure, it appears that Pure Storage isn't getting a disproportionate amount of new business.
  • Expenses are rising. In order to keep innovating merely to retain its current so-so growth rates, the company is needing to invest more heavily into R&D to deliver new products. On top of waning margins, the company is seeing declining operating profits, making the valuation equation for this stock even more difficult.

For a company undergoing operational and macro challenges, I expect to be able to purchase the stock at a valuation discount in order to compensate for the risks. Unfortunately, Pure Storage doesn't trade anywhere cheaply enough to warrant a buy. At current share prices near $55, Pure Storage trades at a market cap of $18.04 billion. After we net off the $1.58 billion in cash and $0.1 billion of debt on Pure Storage's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $16.56 billion.

For FY26 (the year for Pure Storage ending in January 2026), the company has maintained its full-year outlook at $3.515 billion in revenue (+11% y/y) and a 17.0% pro forma operating margin. Wall Street, meanwhile, is expecting $1.71 in pro forma EPS.

This positions Pure Storage's valuation multiples at:

  • 4.7x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 32x FY26 P/E

With downward pressure on margins likely threatening to slow down earnings growth, I don't see the wisdom in paying a >30x earnings multiple for a company that plays in a very competitive industry. I'd take advantage of the opportunity to sell Pure Storage and lock in gains before it begins a deeper correction.

Q1 download

Let's now discuss Pure Storage's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Pure Storage's revenue jumped 12% y/y to $778.5 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $770.2 million (+11% y/y) by a point, while also accelerating slightly over Q4's 11% y/y growth pace.

The company notes that so far, its sales momentum has been un-impacted by the macro environment, though the company is wary that it's entering into a more uncertain landscape that may eventually weigh on sales. Per CEO Charlie Giancarlo's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

As we assess the macro environment, our near-term view for the year remains largely unchanged, although we are navigating increased uncertainty. That said, our consistent performance and disciplined execution will continue to set Pure apart as a leader in our industry. We remain confident in our ability to outpace the competition.

We saw very strong Evergreen//One and Evergreen//Forever sales this past quarter. With tariffs top of mind for many companies, pricing of our Evergreen portfolio will remain unaffected by current tariff related changes. Pure's storage as a service offering, Evergreen//One, delivers the full value of our platform with Pure managing and maintaining the infrastructure with the industry's strongest service level agreements.

In this uncertain tariff environment, our Evergreen model provides customers with pricing predictability, guaranteed SLAs, and a trusted partner committed to transparency. We are confident in our continued momentum to grow market share and strengthen our leadership in data storage and management."

What makes me additionally nervous is the fact that a lot of the demand strength that Pure Storage is seeing right now may be transitory in nature. Meta (META) is a good example: Pure Storage cited its "hyperscale collaboration" with Meta in delivering mass storage solutions for heavy datacenter workloads as one of its core growth drivers. Yet we know that Meta has boosted its near-term capex spending in order to accelerate its investments into AI. Meta is now expecting $64-72 billion in capex spending this year, 7% higher than its previous $60-$65 billion view and more than double at the midpoint versus $37.2 billion in spending in FY24. This doesn't mean, however, that Meta will reliably spend $60+ billion in capex every single year from here on out.

My concern is that Pure Storage is picking up a portion of this elevated near-term spending, both from Meta as well as from smaller peers. This accelerated demand, meanwhile, may rob revenue from future quarters. At the same time, Pure Storage's premium valuation multiples are pricing the stock as if it can continue to grow at this pace ad infinitum.

On the margin side, Pure Storage continued to see sharp y/y headwinds, though conditions improved versus Q4 when the company's gross margin losses spooked the stock market. Q1 gross margins fell -300bps y/y to 70.9%, though that improved 170bps sequentially versus 69.2% in Q4 (which also fell -450bps y/y).

We have a new element to worry about, however, and that's higher opex. Pure Storage is investing heavily in order to remain competitive alongside AI market changes - its R&D spending as a percentage of revenue jumped to 21.4%, 140bps higher than 20.0% in the year-ago Q1.

Combined with the gross margin downsides, we note that Pure Storage's pro forma operating income actually fell -17% y/y to $83 million, with operating margins showing 390bps of y/y decay:

In other words, Pure Storage's double-digit top line growth isn't helping the company deliver the needed economies of scale to start justifying its valuation. The company seems to be benefiting from a massive wave of investments into IT infrastructure and capex to support AI models - but this demand may not be sustainable, and Pure Storage isn't becoming structurally more profitable as a result of its sales growth.

Key takeaways

With elevated valuation multiples, declining gross and operating margins, and the possibility that demand pull-forward is benefiting near-term results at the expense of the longer term, I find very little incentive to buy Pure Storage, especially at a >30x forward P/E ratio. In my view, the stock is primed to disappoint investors, especially if macro headwinds start to impact its sales momentum.

Sell this stock and move to the sidelines.

data.title: From Benchmark To Question Mark: Are U.S. Markets Losing Their Appeal With Investors?
data.description:
U.S. markets have seen multi-year outperformance, but is that trend coming to an end? MoneyTalk discusses with Justin Flowerday, Managing Director and Head of Public Equities with TD Asset Management.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: US markets have seen multiple years of outperformance against their global peers, but with concerns around tariffs and the size of the American deficit, is that trend coming to an end? Joining us now to discuss, Justin Flowerday, Managing Director, Head of Public Equities at TD Asset Management. Always great to have you on the program.

Justin Flowerday: Great to be here, Greg.

Greg Bonnell: So we have seen this outperformance for a long time. There was a lot of factors in place that fed that outperformance. Now there's questions. Tariffs, the Big Beautiful Bill. What are we thinking about America right now?

Justin Flowerday: Yeah. Look, I mean, there's been no shortage of headlines and policy announcements which have kept everyone on their toes. And you mentioned tariffs. I mean, there's been-- all the trade stuff has been absolutely nuts. US dollar weakness has been a theme.

And look, it has people a little bit nervous. And so, year-to-date, the US is flat-ish, as of this morning. And Europe is higher and Asia is higher. So you have seen a little bit of underperformance by US.

I mean, when thinking about what's driven outperformance for the US over the last little while and whether that outperformance is over, which is the question that everyone's asking. I mean, you really do need to think about the driver, which is earnings growth. And over the last three years, over the last 30 years, US earnings growth has been much more superior relative to the rest of the world

And it's because they have a massive innovation engine. And it's fueled by venture capital. And it's fueled by risk taking. And it leads to innovation and the establishment of world-leading technology companies, and communication companies, and healthcare companies that generate above-average margins, above-average returns on equity. I don't think that can be replicated overnight anywhere else in the world.

So the US has had this moment where earnings growth has converged with earnings growth in the rest of the world. Longer term, it still has many levers that will allow it to grow earnings at a good clip.

Greg Bonnell: Sounds to me like you're saying, don't count the US out just yet, based on that strength.

Justin Flowerday: That's my conclusion. Look, short term, anything can happen. And you're clearly getting some headlines and getting announcements that are causing people to reconsider their tactical asset allocation and allocation to the US versus Europe versus Japan. Little things can happen, and there can be short-term outperformance. But over the longer term, that's what I'd say.

Greg Bonnell: So over that shorter and medium term, we have been looking at Europe, other parts of the world. What is the situation in Europe right now? Why is there this interest?

Justin Flowerday: Yeah, so, look, Europe has gone through this period of resurgence and has played catch up. It's been an underperformer for quite some time. And it's played catch up because earnings growth is looking better. In the short term, there's obviously lots of questions around trade and tariffs and what that can do to short-term earnings.

But over the medium term, you've got defense spending, which is going to pick up. Infrastructure spending, which is picking up. And so, lots of demand levers for the overall economy, which will lead to greater demand for companies in Europe and the products and services that they offer.

The other thing I'd mention, though, is when you think about Europe, I mean, this is a group of 27 independent countries which have formed the EU. And as part of that, they have different worldviews, they have different priorities. And so there hasn't always been unity. And I think that lack of unity has led probably to lower productivity growth than they would otherwise have.

And now you're seeing unity. And you're seeing the leaders of these countries trying to get on the same page, in terms of major, major issues that can help that economy going forward. So, Europe is in a better place. I think going forward, they're going to need to find things other than government spending to allow them to reinvigorate the corporate sector.

Greg Bonnell: When you started mentioning about increased defense spending, increased infrastructure to try to boost productivity, made me think about the throne speech. It sounded like a lot of the things that Canada says it wants to do. Are we moving along that same thing as Europe, or do we have the potential in Canada to move along that theme?

Justin Flowerday: Yeah. Look, I think every country in the world, and every company in the world right now, is asking the same questions, which are, how can I do things better, how can I boost productivity in my economy, if you're a country and in your company, if you're a management team? And they're exploring everything. And so things that were off the table politically, let's say, previously are now back on the table.

And it's a good thing because there's a lot of companies and countries who lack that urgency necessary to move the agenda forward and accelerate either GDP growth or earnings growth in the years ahead. So, look, it's all, from my standpoint, very positive. And you're hearing it from companies out there today who are talking about, look, we're being challenged in this way, but it's made us look at this other area of our operation in a different lens. And we're implementing new technology, we're implementing different processes, ways of going, of thinking about our production. And it's all very positive, I think, for longer-term earnings growth.

Greg Bonnell: So government is rethinking, the corporate sector is rethinking how to navigate these waters. As they start to make these pronouncements and start to put these plans in place, how do you see the second half of this year going? It's almost June. Before we know it, we will be in the second half of this year.

Justin Flowerday: Yeah. Yeah. So, the second half is coming quite close. And if we think about some of the things we're looking at, I think I'll point to overall, across sectors, trying to find companies that can maintain margins, right? It can come from having pricing power to be able to increase prices. And so that just comes from brand strength.

The ability to adjust their cost base to protect margins. So that's a general theme. I would say, if you're looking at sectors, there are some sectors that are standing out. And some we've liked for a long time, others that we're starting to pay a little more attention to.

I mean, I'll mention power generation as an example. This is something we've been quite attracted to for quite some time. And the trends there are only getting stronger. And we've got reshoring in the US.

We have a lot of the AI theme that was called into question after DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) in January is now kind of shown that it's, look, this isn't gone. This is actually accelerating. I mean, when you think about the capital spending plans for the large technology companies going into this year, they were looking for about 20% year-on-year growth in capital spending in 2025. That was prior to DeepSeek.

We just heard from them in the last quarter. And when you aggregate the capital spending plans on data center, they're now looking for 35% year-over-year growth. So it's actually accelerating. And as a part of that is just that power generation need, right? At the same time, you're hearing from Trump, we just heard recently on nuclear. And I think he put together three executive orders to accelerate some movement there. Deregulation is moving forward.

So power generation, the fundamentals in that sector are quite attractive. And I think that has legs. And it's not just months of legs. It's quarters and years.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BE Semiconductor: Moving To A 'BUY' (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Dear reader/followers,

I've been following BE Semiconductor (OTC:BESIY) very closely. I've also been investing and making some excellent profits on a trade a few years back in the company, after which I was "out" of the company - which has turned out to be the right choice if you take a peek at the performance, for the past few articles I that I've published. You can find the latest one here, and even if it has generated positive short-term returns since February, I would argue that the company's valuation did not warrant an investment based on this return.

As with any play based on incredible premiumization - meaning premiumization of over 35- 45x P/E, this one is a hard one for me. The ease with which some investors go into these high-P/E investments with the expectations for growth or "to the moon" is never something that I have been able to understand, though it evidently seems to work out quite well for many. Instead, I ground my approach in what I view as sound valuation principles based on historically reasonable numbers and assumptions. And not many companies in sectors or situations like these see a justifiable upside from that.

BESI, at one time, however, was different - and may be different again.

That, and the Q1 2025 period, is why I am giving you my update on the company here.

Let's see what we have as of Q1 2025.

BE Semiconductors trading at 48x P/E normalized - but how can it be a potential buy?

I will be looking at a few different scenarios here to show you the conflict that I, too, am having in trying to estimate where this investment might go. I don't have a conflict of valuation like this lightly - and in fact, other analysts seem somewhat unclear just where to value the company, shifting between "HOLD" and "BUY" targets quite often.

BE Semiconductor is a leader in what is known as atomic layer deposition and advanced packaging equipment. These technologies are crucial for the development of the current generation and the next generation of chips. These companies in the fields, BESI above others, together with ASM, are leaders and crucial players here.

Why is this so important?

We're moving into an age where 3D structures for semiconductor transistors are going to become central. This increases the requirements for precision, reliability, and technological expertise, specifically in atomic layer deposition, where companies like ASM are active. As the limits of transistor scaling become apparent, advanced packaging, which is BESI's bread and butter, is becoming more and more important, because of its enablement of Moore's law - for the time being.

BESI has been laser-focused on working only with leading semiconductor suppliers. This is one of the things I like about the company - it doesn't veer off into left field, and it focuses where its expertise lies, and where, as a result, margins are higher. You can see this in BESI's superb margin levels, both on the top-line gross and lower-level operating and net margins. BESI's focus on what is a high-moat, incredibly niche area where customers more or less have zero choice for what they want to buy and from whom, due to the technical requirements in expertise and focus, means that any new entrant will have an incredible uphill battle.

What I have been insufficiently focusing on in my last articles is the sheer magnitude of BESI's moat in this field. The company has a very clear wide moat, both from the value of its technical expertise, and by what I am assuming would be monumental switching costs for clients from BESI's platforms. The company represents a full-service and vertically integrated shop for advanced packaging, and this is the result of decades of R&D. This has not been said clearly enough previously.

Rather than focusing on transistor size reduction, packaging now focuses on multiple chips to be interconnected. The reaching of Moore's law makes packaging companies, like BESI, an absolute priority because it continues to enable Moore's law - but this has not been the case up until now in the same way because the limits of transistor shrinking have not been reached in the same way until this time.

That's why the bullish case might be that BESI is going to be much more expensive in the future than in the past.

For any company to compete with BESI on a serious level, they would be 20–30 years of R&D behind.

Notions that support the assumption of BESI's high valuation and potential for outperformance are mainly two. Firstly, the company's end markets, semis, have not in any significant way as of this time, built out their hybrid bonding base. Selling time for BESI's products is several times higher than previous models due to technical advancement and more complexity. Because of the lack of choices, I don't see alternatives to BESI continuing to sell at an incredibly high price. This is not an area where you can pay a CNC company to do a "knock-off" that will work as well.

Secondly, and this is a "maybe", the fact that BESI is in the position it's in, means that the day could come where a peer buys BESI. What this would look like is anyone's guess, but given the multiples involved, I wouldn't be surprised at a share price 2-5x the level of the current level, given what's involved. I'm not sure who the acquirer would be, or if BESI's owners even would put the company for sale - but it's an expression of how appealing and how safe you potentially are in this investment. Applied Materials (AMAT) has been increasing its BESI stake, up to 9% as little as a month ago.

What about risks?

What are the risks to such an investment if you were to make it?

The volatility of the semi market is an often-addressed factor that needs to be taken into consideration. It's an indisputably cyclical market, which BESI has experienced. The second factor, and the more important one, is the concentration of income. The loss of a single customer could impact BESI's revenue and earnings growth significantly because of how few companies BESI works with. This also ties in to the unit cost of BESI's new generation, including the Datacon 2200 evo advanced, or the 8800 CHAMEO Advanced, which, compared to older generations, are far more expensive to service and repair. Some players might go for older generations instead.

Then again, in today's IT-oriented world, what company would accept a lower standard and less technologically advanced chips? It's my stance that the companies would instead pass through these costs to their own customers, safeguarding BESI's revenues and customer portfolio.

The recent trading trends and results have done nothing to change my view on the risks for BESI - but a recent deep dive I did, and looking at the future potentials of a BESI investment, have lead me to change my view on the future of the company, where I will change my rating at this time despite what I view as a high valuation.

BESI - The potential for long-term is significant and too good to pass up on

This is a rare valuation case for me. To summarize, I believe the cost of staying out of this company is too high to ignore. A few things have changed my mind on this. First, the company's recent 2024–2025 results, which have been superb, indicate a lower cyclicality due to service income and revenue. Secondly, the AMAT stake, which at 9% implies a significant interest in the company, which I believe will only increase over time as the potential for this market becomes something others focus on.

I believe we're in a situation where we can actually get in on the "ground floor" in a company that at the very least has the potential for a fair value of €180-€200/share, and in a bidding situation or revenue/sales expansion, in a few years could be worth twice that (but that is not my current thesis).

As of right now, we're looking at BESI trading at €108.9/share. That means that even based on my previous target of €110/share, the company is now a "BUY" here.

However, I am significantly increasing my price target. The reason for this significant increase is the potential for 2027-2030 growth in this segment, and unlike in the mentioned riskier plays in the software/AI sector, I do not see the same risk level in this company.

BESI has a yield of 2%, has a near-IG rating of BB+, and an estimated EPS growth rate of almost 40% per year until 2027 (Source: paywalled link F.A.S.T graphs). Using a 5-year average of 35x P/E, that's an upside of over 70% at this time up to the aforementioned fair value of €180/share for the native ticker.

I'm willing to put the company at a 35- 40x P/E for 2027-2028 as a fair value target, which means that the PT for BESI at this time is no longer €110/share for a 15% upside, but rather €140/share. This marks an almost 30% increase in my PT for the long term, not the short term. I recently took a stake in BESI when the company dipped pre-AMAT stake, and I am increasing it. My goal is to get a 2-3% BESI position in my portfolio, as a long-term stake, and get the cost basis as close to double digits as possible. Based on the positives I mentioned above, I believe it is not only within the realm of possibility, but actually likely that the company will reach €180-€200/share in the next 3–4 years. And that's the "base" thesis.

The bullish thesis would be a purchase of BESI, at which point I believe the €180/share fair value is going to be conservative.

My forecast is for a 15-25% revenue growth for 2026 and 2027. This is based on solid growth in servicing as well as sales for advanced packaging equipment, with hybrid bonding being the core advantage and profit driver for the company. I also believe the margins will stay above 60% for the gross level, with operating margins at or below 40%. R&D will remain high, forecast between 10-14%, but this is part of the company's bread and butter.

Beyond the risks mentioned above, I will caution readers that the semiconductor industry is volatile. Demand for chips can change, and BESI's history shows examples of this. As of yet, only 15-18% of BESI's annual revenue is servicing - increasing this will help with the cyclicality. This is why it's crucial, if you invest, that you're open to seeing the long-term thesis, meaning 3 years or more.

Let me state unequivocally and clearly that BESI is now a key long position in my IT/Semi portfolio. Barring chaos and unforeseen events, I see myself continuing to establish a position here, and I view this company as an investment with a long-term upside of between 70-200% in total, depending on the outcome.

BESI should not be ignored here. My new PT for the native ticker comes to a BESIY target of $152/share, which gives the company an excellent upside here.

My thesis for May 2025, and going forward, is as follows.

Thesis

  • BESI is a class-leading and market-leading company in the semi-sector, with several advantages over peers and similar companies. It has excellent RoR and a good yield but needs to be bought at a good valuation to offer a compelling, long-term upside. It can also be sold as a sort of trading play, offering good returns in the short term as well.
  • The current valuation calls for a "BUY" for the company, my PT is €140/share, upgraded from my previous target of about €110/share.
  • I have re-entered the investment and will continue to establish a long position in BESI for the next few years, as what I view as being an incredibly interesting thesis plays out here.

My short-term investment case (6-12 months) is as follows:

Short-term cyclicality prior to a full reversal in the assembly market could, as it has before prove volatile for the company, as it has been proven in the past that volatility is part of this ticker's M.O. I believe it's no longer a reason to stay out of the company for the longer term, only in the short term.

My medium-to-long-term investment case (up to and beyond 5 years) is as follows:

For the long term, I believe there is a very significant upside in the hybrid bonding (HB)/packaging market to where you can get a market-beating result. I am upping my share price target to reflect what I view as a significant potential upside here, and move the company to a "BUY".

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically is enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of €140/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "BUY" rating as of May 2025.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best International Stock Idea investment competition, which runs through June 6. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ahold Delhaize: Marginally Better Value Relatative To Kroger
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, Ahold Delhaize (OTCQX:ADRNY) has significantly outperformed peers such as Carrefour (OTCPK:CRRFY) and Kroger (KR), delivering a return of ~32%:

I previously covered Ahold Delhaize with a Buy rating back in March 2024, highlighting its attractive valuation relative to U.S. peers such as Kroger. At the same time, I did note that European peers such as Carrefour offered an even higher free cash flow yield.

In this article, I share my takeaways from Ahold Delhaize's Q1 2025 results and provide an update on valuation relative to Kroger and Carrefour. I confirm my Buy rating for Ahold Delhaize, largely driven by a more attractive valuation relative to Kroger. Even so, I admit that the Buy case has weakened given the strong appreciation of Ahold Delhaize so far in 2025.

Company Overview

You can access all Ahold Delhaize results on its website here. Ahold Delhaize operates 9,555 stores with a focus on food retail under a variety of brands (Food Lion, Hannaford, Delhaize, etc.). The company reports results in two key segments, the United States at 60% of Q1 2025 net sales and Europe at 40%:

The company's enterprise value totals $49 billion, of which 68% is funded with common equity, leaving a 32% contribution for net debt. We should note that loans account for only 9% of enterprise value, with the remainder of net debt due to lease liabilities.

Q1 2025 results overview

The United States segment's net sales increased 5.2% Y/Y (1.8% in constant currency terms), powered by higher online sales, up 21.8% (17.9% in constant currency). The underlying operating margin slipped by ~0.3% Y/Y to 4.4%, impacted by low margin online and pharmacy sales. As a result, the segment's underlying operating income declined by 0.9% Y/Y (4.1% in constant currency).

Europe segment sales increased 10.1% Y/Y, largely due to the acquisition of Romanian grocery retailer Profi, which closed in the quarter. Excluding this effect, comparable sales increased 3.7% Y/Y. The underlying operating margin increased ~0.3% to 3.4%, driven by strong performance in Benelux. The net effect was a 19.9% Y/Y increase in underlying operating income.

On a consolidated basis, sales increased 7.1% (5% in constant currency), the underlying operating margin slipped by ~0.1% to 3.8%, and the underlying operating income increased by 3.3%. Underlying earnings per share growth was marginally stronger, increasing 4.6% Y/Y to €0.62/share, thanks to ongoing share repurchases:

Overall, I think it is encouraging to see Ahold Delhaize achieving robust online sales growth of 15.4% Y/Y. That said, these are clearly eroding the company's margins. Case in point, the underlying operating income margin stood at 4.3% in 2022, 4.1% in 2023, and 4% in 2024 before slipping to 3.8% in Q1 2025.

Outlook confirmed

Despite the weak margin performance early in 2025, the company did confirm its full-year guidance, expecting a circa 4% underlying operating margin for the full year and free cash flow of at least €2.2 billion:

Most notably, the outlook assumes a EUR/USD exchange rate of 1.10 for the full year. Should current margin weakness/euro strength prove persistent, odds are Ahold Delhaize faces downside risks related to its 2025 outlook. As a reminder, free cash flow stood at €2.4 billion in 2023 and €2.5 billion in 2024, with the +€2.2 billion target for 2025 falling below performance in recent years. As such, despite ongoing sales growth, I think the company will only achieve free cash flows of about €2.3 billion in 2025.

Valuation relative to Kroger

Taking my €2.3 billion free cash flow estimate for 2025, we see that the equity free cash flow yield stands at roughly 6.8%, with each €100 million in free cash flow representing roughly 0.3% in free cash flow yield to equity. Indeed, this is only marginally above Kroger's circa 6.4% free cash flow yield.

However, Ahold Delhaize is spending more on capex, around 3% of sales, compared to only 2.6% capex relative to sales spending at Kroger. This should allow Ahold Delhaize to achieve higher earnings growth in the future.

Last but not least, Ahold Delhaize holds marginally less debt compared to Kroger. Case in point, Ahold Delhaize's financial debt accounts for 9% of enterprise value, compared to circa 21% net debt contribution to enterprise value at Kroger:

Metric\company Ahold Delhaize Kroger
Free cash flow yield to equity, May 2025 6.8% 6.4%
Capex spending relative to sales 2025 3% 2.6%
Net financial debt relative to enterprise value (excluding lease liabilities), May 2025 9% 21%

Source: Author calculations

As such, even after the relative outperformance so far in 2025, I think Ahold Delhaize offers marginally better value compared to Kroger, although the difference is less pronounced compared to my previous article. Even so, I confirm my Buy rating for Ahold Delhaize.

Valuation relative to Carrefour

Ahold Delhaize remains more expensive compared to Carrefour. For instance, Carrefour offers a free cash flow yield to equity of about 15.7%, although it does come with lower capex, only 2.1% relative to sales, and higher net debt (29% of enterprise value):

Metric\company Ahold Delhaize Carrefour
Free cash flow yield to equity, May 2025 6.8% 15.7%
Capex spending relative to sales 2025 3% 2.1%
Net financial debt relative to enterprise value (excluding lease liabilities), May 2025 9% 29%

Source: Author calculations

As such, if you don't mind Carrefour's Brazil/Argentina exposure (which is 25% of Q1 2025 sales), you are most likely better off holding Carrefour over the medium term. On a standalone basis, I would definitely rate Carrefour a Buy.

However, since Europe is not the main market for Ahold Delhaize (i.e. Carrefour is not the best peer), I think sticking with my Buy rating on Ahold Delhaize (when compared to Kroger) is most prudent.

Risks

Looking back to my previous article, it appears Ahold Delhaize's margin weakness is more structural and unlikely to reverse soon. This may result in a prolonged period of weak free cash flow growth, as we have seen since 2023. Eventually, though, the company's high capex spending relative to competitors should pay off, allowing it to stabilize margins and return to free cash flow growth.

Should current dollar weakness continue, Ahold Delhaize's future free cash flow growth will also be impacted in the years ahead. Indeed, if you look at EUR/USD futures, you will see that interest rate differentials predict a circa 2% annual weakening of the U.S. dollar relative to the euro.

Last but not least, my Buy rating is largely based on a marginal discount relative to competitors such as Kroger in Ahold Delhaize's key U.S. market. As shown in the table for Carrefour above, it is quite likely that Carrefour outperforms Ahold Delhaize going forward, even when we factor in the exposure to Latin America.

Conclusion

Ahold Delhaize delivered weak Q1 2025 margins, impacted by growth in low-margin pharmacy and online sales. The company did confirm its full-year guidance, although it most notably assumes an average EUR/USD exchange rate below current levels, pointing to downside risks should current Euro strength continue.

I still think Ahold Delhaize offers marginally better value relative to Kroger, largely thanks to a higher free cash flow yield (despite Ahold Delhaize's higher capex spending) and lower leverage. As such, I confirm my Buy rating for Ahold Delhaize.

Investors should consider risks related to persistent margin weakness, future dollar depreciation relative to the euro, as well as the more compelling valuation of Europe-focused peers such as Carrefour.

Thank you for reading.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
